title: Kitchen Confidential
author: Anthony Bourdain
note: Changed how I think about kitchens and honesty. Bourdain's voice is completely uncompromising. Required reading for anyone who's ever worked a dinner service or just wants to understand what it actually takes.

title: Inspired: How to Create Tech Products Customers Love
author: Marty Cagan
note: The canonical PM text. I re-read chapters of this regularly. 'Discovery' as a formal concept changed how I structure my work.

title: We Fed an Island
author: José Andrés
note: The story of World Central Kitchen's response to Hurricane Maria in Puerto Rico. Operational excellence meets radical generosity. Genuinely moved me.

title: The Mom Test
author: Rob Fitzpatrick
note: Short, practical, and should be mandatory before anyone runs a user interview. The title is annoying. The content is excellent.

title: Salt, Fat, Acid, Heat
author: Samin Nosrat
note: Not a recipe book — a thinking framework for cooking. The four elements aren't a checklist, they're a lens. Applying frameworks like this is the whole game, in kitchens and in product.

title: Thinking in Systems
author: Donella H. Meadows
note: Dense but worth every page. Once you see systems, you can't unsee them. I reference the concept of 'leverage points' constantly.

title: Parable of the Sower
author: Octavia E. Butler
note: Haunting and visionary. Butler wrote this in 1993 and it reads like dispatches from the near future. A masterclass in world-building and character resilience.

title: An Everlasting Meal
author: Tamar Adler
note: Reads more like a philosophy than a cookbook. About making something from whatever you have left. The chapter on eggs alone is worth the whole book.

title: The Hard Thing About Hard Things
author: Ben Horowitz
note: Refreshingly unoptimistic compared to most business books. The section on peacetime vs. wartime CEOs maps surprisingly well to different PM contexts.

title: How to Change Your Mind
author: Michael Pollan
note: Fascinating look at the science and culture of psychedelics. Pollan is one of the best non-fiction writers working. I'm reading this slowly on purpose.
